result:
The first thing to know about running away is that it begins long before you stand at the door with a backpack shaped like a turtle shell.  It begins in the hush between two heartbeats when you realize the second beat sounds exactly like the word “elsewhere.”  I was eight when I heard it, a whisper syncopated under the kitchen table while my mother folded laundry, humming a lullaby pitched slightly off-key, the way loneliness hums when it thinks no one is listening.  The socks became small white flags surrendering to a life I had not yet lived.  I stuffed two into my pockets just in case I needed surrender of my own.

By the time I was twelve I had learned geography from the back of cereal boxes and the aches in my father’s shoulders.  I drew maps on the margins of homework:  here be the dragons of unspent allowance, here the waterfall of my mother’s hidden magazines, here the archipelago of school days that tasted like cardboard.  I labeled the blank middle “The Undiscovered Current of Me,” which was pretentious but felt true the way only pretentious things can when you are twelve and still believe the universe sends invitations.  I waited for mine.  I imagined it engraved on sunrise, delivered by owl, scented like the gum girls older than me traded in bathroom stalls for phone numbers they would never call.

Instead the invitation arrived as a crack.  Not metaphorical—an actual crack, a lightning bolt in the plaster beside my bed one July night when thunder walked across the roof like a drunk deity.  The house exhaled plaster dust.  I tasted it:  chalk and history and the brittle surprise of aging.  I pressed my eye to the fracture and saw another room, smaller, lit by a bulb that flickered Morse code for “now.”  The bulb was naked, obscene, honest.  I decided that when I ran I would travel toward that bulb.  I would ask it what it had seen that made it stutter so.

But running away is also a question of luggage.  At thirteen I practiced in the mirror, arranging my face into expressions that could pass for twenty-one, eighteen at least.  I studied离家出走（lí jiā chū zǒu）in my Chinese-English dictionary: 离家, to leave home; 出走, to walk out.  The hanzi looked like tiny doors propped open by sticks.  I copied them into the palm of my hand with a leaking pen so the doors bled blue.  My mother saw the smudge and asked if I’d been fighting octopi again.  She still spoke to me in the dialect of my younger selves.  I answered in shrug, universal tongue of almost-teenagers.  Inside, the doors throbbed.

I packed hypothetically:  one flashlight, three granola bars, a Swiss-army knife older than I was, the rabbit’s foot key chain my grandfather gave me before forgetting my name, a notebook sealed with duct tape because paper is cowardly and runs its mouth.  I weighed the backpack on the bathroom scale:  7.3 lbs of curated panic.  I practiced walking down the driveway with it, nonchalant whistle, just off to conquer galaxies, don’t mind me.  The neighbor’s sprinklers saluted.  I retreated at the corner where real sidewalks began, afraid the concrete would record footprints that spelled coward.

High school expanded the crack in the wall into a canyon.  The bulb on the other side now pulsed like a disco for insomniacs.  Teachers lectured about the American Dream as if it were a layaway plan:  pay in installments of obedience and someday you’ll own the couch of happiness.  I raised my hand and asked what if you’re allergic to upholstery.  They gave me detention for “smarting off,” which felt like a promotion.  In detention we copied lines from Thoreau:  “The mass of men lead lives of quiet desperation.”  I changed “men” to “kids” and felt the words plank themselves across the canyon, a wobbly bridge.

One afternoon the principal called an assembly about “risk behaviors.”  He wheeled in a wrecked Honda the way pastors wheel in crosses.  He told us the driver had been drinking, speeding, fleeing.  He did not mention what the driver was fleeing toward, only away.  I stared at the crumpled hood and saw origami failed mid-fold.  I touched the scar on my knee shaped like the Milky Way and understood that fleeing is also a collision, just rearranged in time.

I began to practice stillness as a form of escape.  While others raced to promposals and part-time jobs, I sat on the roof outside my window and mapped constellations no one had named yet.  Orion felt too macho, Cassiopeia too vain.  I connected random stars into a pigeon, a bicycle, a bowl of phở.  I labeled the phở constellation “Home But Better,” which made me laugh until I tasted roof tar in my throat.  My phone buzzed with texts:  where r u???  I answered in emoji:  pigeon, bicycle, soup.  No one replied, which proved the language was exclusive, therefore sacred.

College acceptance letters arrived like wedding proposals from strangers.  I spread them on the floor, a paper cemetery of expectations.  Each envelope contained the same subtext:  we believe in your potential as long as you mortgage tomorrow.  I opened none.  Instead I climbed out the window at 2 a.m. with nothing but driver’s license and house key, walked to the 24-hour laundromat because spin cycles sound like ocean and I had never seen the sea.  A woman in flip-flops and a prom dress sobbed into a dryer full of sequins.  I offered her the rabbit’s foot.  She clutched it, asked if I believed in luck.  I said I believed in exits.  She laughed so hard a sequin flew out of her mouth and stuck to my cheek like a tiny moon.  I kept it.  Still have it.  It smells faintly of dryer sheets and someone else’s almost.

At eighteen I finally stepped onto the interstate ramp with thumb extended, backpack upgraded to 12.6 lbs (added:  changes of underwear, zine about anarchist gardening, Polaroid of my parents laughing at a BBQ with their eyes closed).  A semi-truck painted like a whale stopped.  The driver’s name was Lyle; he spoke in roadside haiku:  “Empty coffee cup / sunrise spills across the dash / yesterday’s perfume.”  I nodded as if fluent.  He told me he once drove through a tornado and the center was filled with birthday balloons.  I asked what happened after.  He said the balloons popped like secrets and the sky rained rubber.  I wanted to ride forever but he dropped me at a truck stop shaped like a teapot in Nebraska because everyone needs to be poured out somewhere.

The teapot had showers that cost six minutes per quarter.  I spent two dollars and emerged pink as dawn.  A man sold jewelry out of a briefcase:  class rings no one claimed, initials that spell nobody.  I traded the Swiss-army knife for one engraved “To J, Forever.”  I slipped it onto the chain with the rabbit’s foot.  Now I had three feet:  flesh, luck, forever.  I felt taller.

I worked odd jobs the way some people collect passport stamps.  Detasseling corn:  the rows are tall enough to drown in, the tassels are yellow as old teeth, the days taste like aluminum.  Picking blueberries:  fingers stained the color of bruise, stomach ache from quality control, the bushes humming with bees who have no union.  Painting barns:  the splatter on my arms like constellations of cream, the homeowner offering lemonade mixed with something colorless that burns, the ladder wobbling like my conviction.  Each paycheck I sent half home in envelopes with no return address, a reverse ransom.

Somewhere in Kansas I met a girl named Tuesday who claimed she was born on a Wednesday but refused to be predictable.  She wore men’s dress shirts repurposed into dresses, the cuffs fluttering like bored flags.  We slept in an abandoned grain elevator, the walls curved like the inside of a cello.  She played music by tapping spoons on rusted bolts:  “Concerto for Nowhere in B minor.”  We kissed and tasted metal.  She asked where I was going.  I said toward the bulb that flickers.  She said take me, I’m afraid of the dark.  We walked together three weeks until a dust storm rose like orange judgment and when it settled she was gone, leaving only a spoon bent into a question mark.  I hung it from the rabbit-foot chain.  It clinked against J’s ring like small applause.

At twenty-two I reached the coast, but it was the wrong one—Atlantic instead of Pacific, rocky instead of dreamy.  The ocean smelled like expired soup.  I took a room above a seafood restaurant that played whale songs to mask the screams of lobsters.  Every night I dreamed of butter.  I wrote letters to Tuesday and taped them to pigeons who couldn’t read.  They returned envelopes full of breadcrumbs and regret.  I began to forget the crack in my childhood wall, the bulb beyond.  I began to believe I had imagined them, the way one imagines an appendix:  present but purposeless.

One winter the restaurant closed.  The owner handed me a crate of misfit objects left by customers:  single mittens, a diary with pages glued shut, a snow globe containing what looked like cornflakes.  I took them to the pier and arranged them like a museum of almost.  A boy skateboarded past, stopped, asked if I was selling.  I said I was curating.  He offered a joint and a story:  his mother once tried to mail herself to Switzerland but customs sent her back postage due.  We laughed until the joint became a comma between our sentences.  He said the world is a run-on sentence.  I said I was searching for the period.  He kissed me soft as permission.  His name was Marco; he smelled like coconut and grind.  We moved into a squat with twenty strangers and one functioning toilet named Godot.  Godot ran only cold, which felt theological.

We made art from dumpster gold:  chandeliers of cracked CDs, murals of coffee grounds, a sculpture of plastic forks titled “Family Dinner.”  Tourists paid ten bucks to Instagram themselves beside it.  We bought groceries, repaired the roof, taught each other how to tie knots that could hold a life.  Marco and I learned to cook paella without shellfish, substituting rice with stories.  The squat became a pocket universe, stitched together by dental floss and delusion.  I stopped flinching from sirens.  The rabbit’s foot, the ring, the spoon clinked like small change in a country that bartered in wonder.

Yet the bulb kept flickering in dreams, Morse code upgraded to high-def.  It spelled:  “enough.”  I argued:  enough what?  Silence.  One dawn I woke to find Marco gone, a note scraped into frost on the window:  “Went to see the sentence through.  Keep the fork.”  I wore it like a necklace.  The squat dissolved into arguments about whose turn to feed Godot.  I packed 15.4 lbs this time:  added the unmailed diary, the cornflake snow globe, a postcard of a lighthouse I intended to become.

I headed west finally, hitchhiked with a woman trucking solar panels.  She said every panel was a sun captured and domesticated.  I asked if we were doing the sun a favor or committing kidnapping.  She laughed so hard the rig swerved into the mellow part of the dotted line.  She dropped me at the edge of desert where the sand looked like crushed diamonds and the air tasted like apology.  I walked until my footprints became a manuscript no one would publish.  At night the stars screamed brilliance.  I found a town consisting of three gas stations and an opera house.  Inside, the seats were velvet the color of dried blood.  A single tenor rehearsed “Nessun dorma” to rows of ghosts.  I sat.  He paused, said I was the first audience in ten years.  I applauded; the sound ricocheted like trapped doves.  He offered me the role of Turandot if I promised to hit the high B flat of longing.  I said I’d try but my throat was full of forks.  He handed me a pitch pipe shaped like a key.  I blew; the note came out cracked but honest.  He bowed.  We performed that night to lizards and tumbleweed.  The review in the local paper read:  “Weather was fine.”

I stayed six months, learned to project grief into balcony seats, to vibrate my sternum until the rafters remembered flight.  Between arias I listened for the bulb.  It had gone silent, perhaps hoarse from years of warning.  I feared I had outrun it, or worse, replaced it with footlights.  One evening a dust devil spun itself into the shape of Tuesday.  It whispered, “Exit, pursued by meaning.”  I took the hint.  I left the pitch pipe on the stage, a key with no lock.

I crossed the mountains in a school bus painted by retirees who believed rainbows could be reverse-engineered.  They played ukuleles and served mushroom tea that tasted like forgiveness.  The peaks wore snow like lies they couldn’t quit.  I scribbled postcards to Marco, Tuesday, Lyle, the tenor, Godot:  “Wish you were here, wish I were there, wish wishes had ankles.”  I addressed them to the crack in my old bedroom wall, zip code of elsewhere.  The retirees dropped me at the edge of the Pacific finally, the correct ocean, the one that ended in sunsets instead of syntax.

The water was colder than advertised, the color of unmailed letters.  I walked the tide line collecting stones shaped like former hearts.  I built a cairn spelling “almost” then kicked it over.  I needed a new alphabet.  I found a job rehabilitating injured seabirds.  Pelicans with wings snapped by fishermen’s pride, gulls poisoned by greed wrapped in six-pack rings.  I learned to hold panic in a towel, to straighten feathers with tweezers, to force-feed anchovies like terrible secrets.  The birds bit, shat, healed.  When released they did not thank me; they simply remembered how to be horizon.  I envied their amnesia.

One spring evening I carried a pelican to the edge.  It hesitated at the brink between sand and flight, turned its dinosaur eye on me.  In the black pupil I saw the bulb, flickering now steady, white and round as moon.  It did not spell; it simply was.  I understood it had never been elsewhere, it had been waiting for me to stop running toward and start running with.  I opened my palm.  The pelican launched, wings blocking the sun like a period at the end of a very long clause.  The cuffs of my shirt—once Tuesday’s, then Marco’s, then mine—flapped applause.  The rabbit’s foot, the ring, the spoon, the fork jingled their small change anthem.  I felt the weight adjust:  7.3 lbs again, precisely the heft of eight-year-old possibility.

I returned to the squat though it had been gentrified into a quinoa dispensary.  I stood outside, pressed my hand to the brick.  Somewhere inside Godot still waited, probably charging rent.  I walked to the pier where the crate of misfits once waited.  The boy on the skateboard was now a man pushing a stroller.  He did not recognize me; I did not expect him to.  I gave the baby the cornflake snow globe.  She shook it, laughed at the blizzard of breakfast.  I hailed a rideshare to the interstate.  The driver asked destination.  I said, “Back to the first sentence.”  He said that’ll cost extra.  I paid in stories; he accepted, eyes wet in the rearview.

We arrived at the house with the crack.  My parents had moved; the new owners grew tomatoes in the front yard, red as apologies.  I knocked.  They let me in because I wore politeness like a badge.  The crack had been painted over, but paint is a liar.  I touched the wall; it trembled.  Upstairs, a child’s voice hummed off-key.  I left before introduction.  Outside, I planted the bent spoon question mark in the soil near the tomatoes.  Maybe it will grow into an answer.  Maybe just rust.  Either way, the cycle photosynthesizes.

The ride back to the coast took forty years compressed into one sunset.  I carried no backpack now, only pockets.  Inside:  the sequin moon, the rabbit’s foot bleached to honesty, the ring still promising forever to J who never collected, the fork now tuned to key of C.  I weigh nothing on departure scales.  The ocean greets me like an old squat mate who owes rent and brings wine.  We sit.  I remove my shoes, let the tide erase footprints before I make them.  The bulb, long internalized, steadies its beam across the water.  It does not say enough; it says begin.  I wade in up to ankles, calves, heart.  The birds wheel overhead, spelling migration in a language I finally comprehend:  every arrival is a departure refusing to admit it.  I dive.  The water is cold, then kind.  Somewhere a semi-truck painted like a whale honks Morse for happy birthday.  Balloons of breath escape my mouth, rise, pop into rubbery stars.  I surface laughing, tasting salt and anchovy and tomorrow.  The current takes me—not away, but with.  The word elsewhere has finally found its verb.  I conjugate:  I am elsewhering.  You are elsewhering.  We are elsewhering together, a continent of motion, a nation of cracks who learned to whistle light.  The 2500th word is the first heartbeat of the next run, and it sounds exactly like here.

Inside:  the sequin moon, the rabbit’s foot bleached to honesty, the ring still promising forever to J who never collected, the fork now tuned to key of C.  I weigh nothing on departure scales.  The ocean greets me like an old squat mate who owes rent and brings wine.  We sit.  I remove my shoes, let the tide erase footprints before I make them.  The bulb, long internalized, steadies its beam across the water.  It does not say enough; it says begin.  I wade in up to ankles, calves, heart.  The birds wheel overhead, spelling migration in a language I finally comprehend:  every arrival is a departure refusing to admit it.  I dive.  The water is cold, then kind.  Somewhere a semi-truck painted like a whale honks Morse for happy birthday.  Balloons of breath escape my mouth, rise, pop into rubbery stars.  I surface laughing, tasting salt and anchovy and tomorrow.  The current takes me—not away, but with.  The word elsewhere has finally found its verb.  I conjugate:  I am elsewhering.  You are elsewhering.  We are elsewhering together, a continent of motion, a nation of cracks who learned to whistle light.  The 2500th word is the first heartbeat of the next run, and it sounds exactly like here.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
